title: Maharashtra Rent Control Act — tenant protection against eviction and rent increase
text: The Maharashtra Rent Control Act 1999 provides strong protections to tenants in Maharashtra against arbitrary eviction and unfair rent increases. Under Section 15, no landlord shall recover possession of any premises or part thereof except in execution of a decree or order made by the court. A landlord who evicts a tenant without a court order commits an offence. Under Section 16, a landlord can seek eviction only on specific grounds including: the tenant has not paid rent for six months, the tenant has sublet without permission, the tenant has used the premises for a purpose other than the agreed purpose, the tenant has caused nuisance, the landlord requires the property for genuine personal use, or the building requires demolition for reconstruction. The mere fact that the landlord wishes to sell the property is not a ground for eviction under the Maharashtra Rent Control Act. Under the Act, a landlord cannot increase rent by more than the permitted amount. Any demand for rent increase beyond the permitted limit is illegal and the tenant can refuse to pay. The tenant can apply to the Rent Control Court if the landlord demands excess rent. The Rent Control Court has jurisdiction over all residential and commercial premises covered by the Act. A tenant can apply to the Rent Control Court for restoration of possession if illegally evicted. The Act also protects tenants from harassment — a landlord who deliberately cuts off water, electricity or other amenities to force a tenant to vacate commits an offence under the Act.
